Both choices require explicit scientific justification given that more current ensembles and scenarios exist and are in wide use."},"clarification_needed":false,"clarification_question":""}

rationale.R1.1: The contribution framing ('fundamental shift,' 'new standard') warrants scrutiny against what is actually delivered; the gap statement is implicit and the thesis needs sharpening to match the evidence scope.
rationale.R1.2: The inferential chain from a 6.2% compute reduction with a single baseline to 'fundamental shift' and 'new state of the art' is logically strained; cross-section coherence between the methods description and the conclusions paragraph is weak.
rationale.R1.3: Load-bearing: multiple strong claims ('new state of the art,' 'new standard,' 'fundamental shift,' 'substantial advance') rest on a single-baseline comparison (uniform subsampling), a modest compute reduction, and a held-out query set drawn from one national assessment; no comparison to other learned-routing or surrogate-model methods is offered.
rationale.R1.4: Novelty is asserted broadly but benchmarked only against uniform sampling; the absence of comparison to other cost-aware climate inference methods leaves the SotA claim unsubstantiated and the delta vs. prior work unclear.
rationale.R1.7: CMIP5 (2012) is designated the gold-standard reference while CMIP6 is relegated to an appendix sanity check; IPCC AR5 (2014) serves as the scenario backbone rather than AR6 (2021). Both choices require explicit scientific justification given that more current ensembles and scenarios exist and are in wide use.
